text: Though I'm sharing a robotic cholecystectomy, this is approximately three months after percutaneous cholecystostomy tube in an elderly patient with a lot of comorbidities and anticoagulation at the time that they came in very ill with their cholecystitis. The inflammation actually really wasn't that bad. I've already removed the drain.

The surgeon separates the drain tract and begins working towards the triangle of Calot. The gallbladder wall is pulled down, and the surgeon attempts to separate it from the duodenum. The common bile duct is visible in the surgical field.

I've been leaning towards dissecting more and more with the scissors. I'm just so used to the hook laparoscopically, I do have it available, but you'll see later in the case I switch back to scissors. And I do think that I'm probably going to transition to all scissors in the future to avoid the need for additional instruments.
The surgeon identifies the common duct and proceeds to remove fat from the inferior edge of the gallbladder.
The surgeon switches back to using the hook instrument and begins dissecting along the lateral or right side of the gallbladder, carefully working along the lateral edge behind the gallbladder.

The surgeon identifies a posterior artery running alongside the bipolar instrument and decides to move to the medial aspect. An anterior branch of the artery is visible, and a large veil of fat covers the anterior wall of the gallbladder, which is being dissected. The surgeon observes a false green hue through the peritoneal veil, initially suspecting an aberrant duct, but confirms it is not present.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the medial edge of the gallbladder, honing in on the cystic duct. The dissection progresses along the artery, maneuvering over it due to the positioning of the gallbladder.

The surgeon creates a window behind the gallbladder, enhancing the exposure for establishing the critical view. The gallbladder is dissected from top down, leaving the artery and duct clearly visible for further work.

The surgeon identifies the anterior and posterior arteries attached to the gallbladder and begins dissecting behind them to access the common channel, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon uses scissors to dissect beneath the artery, causing some bleeding. The dissection focuses on creating a window around the gallbladder, with visible bleeding contained within the gallbladder area.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind both the anterior and posterior arteries, enlarging the window to facilitate clip placement. The cystic duct is examined, and adventitial fibrovascular fatty tissue is thinned to ensure clips can be applied effectively. The artery is prioritized for removal to simplify subsequent clip placement.

The surgeon enlarges the window behind the gallbladder to accommodate three clips, securing both the anterior and posterior branches of the artery together. The cystic duct is thinned out further to create a suitable landing zone for clip placement.

The surgeon uses scissors to perform dissection around the cystic duct and artery, utilizing the pointed nature of the scissors to access tight areas. The surgeon assesses whether the dissection is sufficient for clip placement.
The surgeon positions clips on the cystic duct, which has been cleared of excess fat, ensuring a secure placement on the true cystic duct wall.
And that essentially completes the operation. Hope you enjoyed. Comments and suggestions are welcome.
